passageSets.title: The Threads of Community
passageSets.passage:
When social theorists speak of 'social capital', they mean to capture an intuition older than the phrase itself: a person embedded in a dense web of relationships possesses something valuable that a comparably talented isolate does not. The metaphor of capital is deliberate. Like money, the bonds of trust and reciprocity that link neighbors can be accumulated, invested, and drawn upon in times of need. A community rich in such ties, the argument runs, polices itself more gently, raises its children more attentively, and rebounds from misfortune more quickly. The thesis is attractive, and real evidence supports it. Yet the metaphor conceals as much as it reveals, and the enthusiasm it inspires deserves a more skeptical hearing than it usually receives.

Consider first what the concept gets right. Where neighbors know one another, doors are watched, favors are exchanged without contracts, and information about jobs or trustworthy plumbers travels along lines of acquaintance. Such networks lower the friction of daily life and supply a kind of insurance that no formal institution fully replaces. But notice that these benefits flow most reliably within tightly knit groups whose members already resemble one another. The very density that makes a clan or a congregation supportive also makes it suspicious of outsiders, resistant to novelty, and quick to punish the member who strays. Solidarity, in other words, is frequently purchased at the price of conformity, and the warmth a community extends to its own is often the same heat it turns against the stranger.

The economic metaphor introduces a second distortion. Capital, properly speaking, is fungible and transferable; a sum saved by one person can be lent to another and repaid with interest. Trust behaves nothing like this. It cannot be handed to a stranger, cannot be stockpiled in advance of a particular relationship, and frequently evaporates the moment it is treated as an instrument. The neighbor who helps only because she expects a calculated return has already ceased to be the kind of neighbor the theory celebrates. By dressing fragile human attachments in the language of accounting, the metaphor tempts us to imagine that communities can be engineered, that trust can be deposited by policy, and that what took generations to grow might be installed in an afternoon.

None of this means the concept should be discarded. It means it should be handled with care. When commentators lament a decline in associational life and propose to revive it, they are right that isolation carries genuine costs, but wrong to assume that more connection is always better. A neighborhood thick with informal surveillance can shelter the vulnerable or it can enforce a stifling uniformity; a network that channels opportunity to insiders is, from the outside, simply a wall. The honest analyst must therefore ask not merely how much social capital a place possesses, but what kind, on whose behalf it operates, and who is quietly left beyond its reach.

Seen this way, social capital is neither the civic panacea its champions advertise nor the illusion its critics allege. It is a description of a real resource that, like most resources, is distributed unequally and wielded for ends both generous and cruel. To celebrate the bonds of community without examining their boundaries is to admire a fortress for its walls while forgetting that walls, by their nature, keep some people out.

passageSets.title: The Office and Its Forms
passageSets.passage:
No institution is more thoroughly despised in casual conversation, or more indispensable in practice, than the bureaucracy. We curse the forms, the queues, and the unbending clerk who cannot make an exception, yet the same apparatus that frustrates us also delivers our mail, certifies our doctors, and ensures that the bridge we cross was inspected by someone accountable. To understand the modern world is, in large part, to understand why this form of organization triumphed over older ways of getting things done. Its victory was not an accident, nor merely the scheme of power-hungry administrators. Bureaucracy spread because, measured against the personal favoritism and improvisation it replaced, it was demonstrably more rational, more predictable, and more fair.

The genius of the bureaucratic ideal lies in its impersonality. Decisions are to be made according to written rules rather than the mood of the official, applied to cases by their category rather than the identity of the petitioner. The same documents, filed by anyone, should yield the same result. This is why the office treats us as cases rather than as persons, and although that treatment can feel cold, it is the very source of a hard-won equality. A system that decides by rule cannot easily be bribed, cannot lawfully prefer the well-born to the obscure, and leaves behind a paper trail by which its errors can be challenged. The faceless quality we resent is inseparable from the protection it affords: where everything is personal, the powerful always win.

And yet the triumph carries a shadow. The same rules that guarantee fairness also bind the official who would do good, for the clerk who bends a regulation to help a sympathetic case has, by that act, reintroduced the arbitrariness the system was built to abolish. Worse, an apparatus designed as a means has a tendency to become an end in itself. Procedures multiply, the file grows more important than the person it describes, and officials measure their success by whether the forms were completed correctly rather than whether anyone was actually helped. The structure that liberated us from personal domination threatens to enclose us in what one famous critic called an iron cage, a world so thoroughly ordered that spontaneity, judgment, and even ordinary kindness come to seem like irregularities to be stamped out.

The temptation, having named this danger, is to call for the abolition of red tape and the restoration of human discretion. But this is where clear thinking is most needed, because discretion is exactly what bureaucracy was invented to constrain. Grant the official freedom to judge each case on its merits, and you have also granted the freedom to favor friends, to extract bribes, and to indulge prejudice under the cover of compassion. The dilemma is genuine and admits no tidy escape. We cannot have the fairness of rules without their rigidity, nor the warmth of discretion without its capricious abuses.

To live in a modern society, then, is to make an uneasy peace with the office and its forms. The mature response is neither to romanticize the personal world bureaucracy displaced nor to pretend the cage does not chafe. It is to recognize that we have traded one set of burdens for another, and to remain vigilant that the means we built to serve us does not quietly become our master.

passageSets.title: The Verdict of Nature
passageSets.passage:
We like to believe that our tastes are the most personal thing about us, welling up from some inner spring of sensibility that no one taught and no one could dictate. The person who prefers an austere documentary to a lavish blockbuster, or unsalted bread to sweet pastry, experiences that preference as simply who he is. A sociology of taste begins by treating this feeling of spontaneity with polite suspicion. It does not deny that preferences feel natural; it asks why they feel natural, and why, when we map them across a population, they fall into such suspiciously regular patterns. For if taste were truly idiosyncratic, it would scatter at random. Instead it clusters by education, by occupation, and above all by the conditions in which a person was raised.

The explanation is that taste is learned so early and so thoroughly that it ceases to feel like learning at all. A child raised among books, concerts, and parents who discuss the merits of a building absorbs not merely information but a whole disposition, a way of holding the body, a confidence about which fork to use and which opinions to voice. This inherited competence, acquired without conscious effort, later passes for natural refinement. The crucial point is that it is not distributed equally. Families with resources transmit it as surely as they transmit property, so that the child of cultivated parents arrives at school already fluent in the very sensibilities the school will reward. What looks like raw talent is frequently a head start, and what looks like the absence of taste is frequently the absence of an expensive education.

Once this is grasped, the social function of taste comes into view. Preferences do not merely express who we are; they sort us, and they justify the sorting. To call another person's enthusiasm vulgar, or his furniture gaudy, is to claim a superiority that feels aesthetic but operates as social. The genius of the system is that it never announces itself as a hierarchy of power. The privileged do not say that their position rests on inherited advantage; they say, and sincerely believe, that they simply have better taste. Inequality is thereby laundered into merit. The arrangement that benefits them is experienced by everyone, winners and losers alike, as the natural order of the discerning and the crude.

This analysis can be pushed too far, and honesty requires acknowledging its limits. To explain how a taste is acquired is not to prove that the object is worthless; a symphony does not become noise merely because appreciating it was a privilege. Nor are people the helpless puppets of their upbringing, for tastes shift, travel across the old boundaries, and are sometimes deliberately remade. Still, these qualifications do not rescue the comfortable belief we began with. They show only that the system is more porous than a crude account suggests, not that taste is the innocent, purely private affair it pretends to be.

The next time we are tempted to despise another's pleasures, then, the more searching question is not what their taste reveals about them, but what our contempt reveals about us. To recognize that our refinements were taught is not to abandon them. It is only to surrender the flattering illusion that in preferring what we prefer, we are registering the verdict of nature rather than the accident of our birth.

passageSets.title: The Bonds of the Gift
passageSets.passage:
Nothing seems more spontaneous than a gift. We imagine it as the pure opposite of a transaction, given freely, with no thought of return, an expression of affection unsullied by calculation. Anthropology has long regarded this picture as charming but misleading. Across a remarkable range of societies, the giving of gifts turns out to be one of the most rule-bound activities human beings undertake, governed by obligations so powerful that they can hardly be refused. To accept a gift is to incur a debt; to fail to reciprocate is to confess inferiority or to insult the giver. Far from standing outside the world of exchange, the gift is one of its oldest and most binding forms, and its apparent freedom is precisely the disguise that allows it to work.

The pattern was famously summarized as a triple obligation: to give, to receive, and to repay. Each link in this chain is enforced not by written law but by something more insistent, the threat of shame and the promise of honor. A person of standing must give, for generosity is the visible proof of status; to hoard is to shrink in the eyes of the community. The recipient, in turn, can rarely decline without giving offense, since refusal implies a suspicion of the giver or a refusal of the relationship itself. And having received, one is bound to return something of comparable worth, lest the original gift harden into a permanent mark of dependence. Through this circulation of objects, alliances are forged, rivalries are conducted, and the social order is continually rewoven.

It would be sentimental, then, to treat gift-giving as simply the warm alternative to cold commerce. The gift can be a weapon as easily as an embrace. To give more than a rival can possibly return is to humiliate him, to assert dominance under the guise of kindness; some societies elaborated this into competitive feasts in which leaders vied to give away, or even destroy, staggering quantities of goods. The lavish host purchases prestige, and the guest who cannot match him is diminished. Even in ordinary life, the generous benefactor and the perpetual debtor are bound in a relationship that is anything but equal. Generosity, in short, is among the subtlest instruments of power, all the more effective because it wears the face of selflessness.

What, then, of our own conviction that a true gift expects nothing back? This ideal is itself a cultural product, the achievement of societies that have walled off a sphere of pure commerce, leaving the gift to carry the meanings that money is forbidden to express. Yet even here the old logic persists beneath the etiquette. We keep silent accounts of who gave what, feel the sting of a present unreciprocated, and bristle at the friend who offers cash where a gift was expected, precisely because cash makes the exchange too honest. The modern free gift is less an escape from obligation than a more discreet way of honoring it.

To study the gift is therefore to watch self-interest and solidarity perform an intricate dance in which neither ever quite leaves the floor. The objects we exchange are never merely objects; they are bearers of relationship, carrying with them a piece of the giver and a claim upon the receiver. In learning to read them, we learn something uncomfortable and clarifying about ourselves.

choices.text: argue that gift-giving, far from being purely free and spontaneous, is governed by binding social obligations

choices.label: B
choices.text: explain how to choose appropriate gifts for different occasions

choices.label: C
choices.text: prove that all gifts are ultimately disguised commercial transactions identical to buying and selling

choices.label: D
choices.text: trace the history of money and its replacement of barter

questions.explanation: A is correct because the passage repeatedly contrasts the naive picture of the free gift with the anthropological finding that gifts impose powerful obligations to give, receive, and repay. B is wrong because it offers no practical gift-selection advice; it analyzes the social logic of exchange. C is wrong because the author distinguishes gifts from pure commerce, noting that moderns wall off a sphere of pure commerce precisely so gifts can carry meanings money cannot, so gifts are not identical to buying and selling. D is wrong because money appears only as a contrast, and the passage is not a history of currency or barter.

passageSets.title: Before We Judge
passageSets.passage:
Few ideas have done more to civilize the study of other peoples than cultural relativism, and few have been more thoroughly abused. In its original and defensible form, it is a principle of method rather than of morals. It instructs the observer to understand a practice in the context of the whole way of life that gives it meaning, before rushing to praise or condemn. A custom that strikes the outsider as bizarre, even repellent, may prove on closer study to be reasonable given the beliefs and circumstances of those who hold it. This discipline of suspended judgment was a hard-won corrective to centuries of complacent contempt, in which travelers measured every foreign society against their own and unfailingly found it wanting.

The trouble begins when this useful rule of inquiry hardens into a sweeping doctrine about value. From the sound observation that we should understand before we judge, some have drawn the very different conclusion that we may never judge at all, that every practice is valid within its own culture and that no standard exists by which one could ever be criticized from outside. This is a leap, not a deduction, and it leads quickly into difficulty. If each culture is the sole author of its own morality, then the reformer who fights an injustice within her own society is merely a deviant, and the outsider who objects to cruelty is guilty of nothing worse than bad manners. A doctrine meant to cultivate humility ends by disarming conscience.

The doctrine also rests on a picture of culture that anthropology itself has abandoned. It imagines each society as a sealed, internally uniform unit, every member of which subscribes to a single shared code. But real cultures are nothing so tidy. They are sites of argument, riven by dissent, containing rebels and reformers, the powerful and those they oppress. To say that a practice is simply what a culture endorses is usually to mistake the voice of its rulers for the consent of all its members. The woman who chafes against a custom that confines her, the heretic who questions the priests, are as much a part of the culture as the traditions they resist. Whose version, then, are we forbidden to criticize?

The way out is to keep the method while refusing the dogma. We can insist, as the early relativists did, on the strenuous effort to understand, and still hold that understanding does not oblige us to approve. Indeed the two are perfectly compatible: it is often only after we grasp why a practice persists that our criticism becomes accurate rather than reflexive, aimed at the real injury rather than at the merely unfamiliar. Suspending judgment is a discipline appropriate to the early stages of inquiry, not a vow to be kept forever. The anthropologist who comes to understand a community earns, rather than forfeits, the right to an opinion about it.

Cultural relativism, rightly understood, is thus a gateway and not a destination. It opens the mind that ethnocentrism had slammed shut, and asks us to look long and patiently before we speak. What it cannot do, without collapsing into incoherence, is forbid us to speak at all. The humility it teaches is real; the paralysis some have drawn from it is a misunderstanding we are free, and sometimes obliged, to refuse.

B is wrong because he argues that the all-standards-equally-valid position disarms conscience and is incoherent, the opposite of endorsing it. passageSets.title: Strangers in the Crowd
passageSets.passage:
To move from a village to a great city is to undergo a transformation that residents of cities rarely notice, because they are immersed in it, and that newcomers feel as a peculiar mixture of exhilaration and loss. In the village, one is known. Every face belongs to a name, a family, a reputation accumulated over years; one is watched, judged, and supported by people who will be there tomorrow. The city dissolves this web. Among millions of strangers, one becomes, for most practical purposes, invisible, a passing figure whom no one will remember and no one can hold to account. This anonymity is the defining social fact of urban life, and almost everything distinctive about the character of the city flows, for better and for worse, from it.

Consider first what anonymity gives. To be unknown is to be free of the village's relentless surveillance, free to reinvent oneself, to hold unpopular beliefs, to love whom one chooses, to fail in private and begin again. The city has always been a refuge for those whom small communities found intolerable: the eccentric, the dissenter, the artist, the person whose very existence offended the neighbors. Where no one is watching, a thousand experiments in living become possible that the village would have crushed at birth. This is no small thing. Much of what we prize as modern tolerance is less a triumph of enlightened attitudes than a byproduct of scale, of the simple fact that in a crowd of strangers, one's neighbors lack both the knowledge and the motive to interfere.

But the same condition exacts a price that the celebrants of urban freedom prefer to overlook. The person no one will hold to account is also the person no one will catch when she falls. Bombarded by more faces, claims, and stimuli than any mind can absorb, the city dweller learns to filter, to look past the stranger, to cultivate a studied indifference, the famous detachment of the urbanite. This reserve is not cruelty; it is a defense, the only way to remain sane amid a flood of demands that one could not possibly answer. Yet a defense practiced daily becomes a habit, and the habit of not-seeing can curdle into a genuine incapacity to care. The crowd that liberates is the same crowd in which a person may collapse on the pavement and be stepped over for an hour.

It is tempting to take sides, to mourn the lost warmth of the village or to celebrate the city's emancipation, but both responses are too simple. Freedom and indifference are not separable goods and evils that we might somehow keep apart; they are two names for a single thing, the loosening of the bonds that once both constrained and held us. The reserve that lets me ignore the suffering stranger is the same reserve that lets the stranger live unbothered by me. We cannot, in honesty, demand the privacy without accepting the solitude, or enjoy the tolerance while lamenting the cold.

The city, then, is not a fall from the village's grace, nor an escape from its prison, but a different bargain, with its own gains and its own griefs. To understand it is to hold both halves of that bargain in view at once, and to resist the comfort of pretending we could have the one without the other.

passageSets.title: Many Ways to Be Kin
passageSets.passage:
Of all the institutions we are tempted to call natural, the family seems the most obviously rooted in biology. A mother, a father, their children: what could be more universal, more given by nature, than this? Anthropology has spent more than a century complicating that confident picture, and its findings are among the discipline's most quietly subversive. Kinship, it turns out, is not a simple reflection of the facts of reproduction. It is a system of socially recognized relationships, and the societies of the world have recognized them in a startling variety of ways. The biological facts are everywhere the same; the meanings a society builds upon them are not, and it is the meanings, not the biology, that determine who counts as kin.

The evidence is abundant and, to the untraveled imagination, astonishing. In some societies a child belongs to the line of the mother and regards the biological father almost as a guest, while authority passes from a man to his sister's sons rather than his own. Elsewhere the word we would translate as father is applied equally to a man and to all his brothers, and a child grows up with many fathers and mothers in a sense no less real for being unfamiliar to us. There are societies in which a woman may take a wife, becoming the recognized father of the children that wife bears, and others in which the dead are reckoned among the living members of a household. Each arrangement is coherent, workable, and to its members as obvious and proper as ours is to us.

What such variety reveals is that kinship systems are solving social problems, not merely recording biological ones. Every society must decide how property will pass between generations, who owes care and loyalty to whom, which unions are permitted and which forbidden, and how the group will renew its alliances with outsiders. The diverse forms of the family are different answers to these enduring questions. A rule that channels inheritance through the mother's line, for instance, is not a quaint error about paternity; it is a coherent solution to the problem of keeping property within a stable group. Once we see kinship as a tool for organizing cooperation across time, its variety stops looking like confusion and starts looking like ingenuity.

The harder and more valuable step is to turn this lens upon ourselves. If the kinship systems of distant peoples are cultural constructions, then so is ours, however natural it feels. The notion that a family consists of two parents and their biological offspring, living apart from other relatives, is not a deliverance of nature but a particular historical arrangement, suited to particular economic conditions and far from universal even in our own past. To recognize this is not to declare our arrangement worthless; it is to strip it of the false authority of nature. Practices we defend as simply the way families are turn out to be the way our families have lately chosen to be, which is a humbler and more honest claim.

The study of kinship thus performs the classic service of anthropology. It takes what seemed fixed and shows it to be made, takes what seemed natural and shows it to be chosen. In doing so it widens the sense of the possible, reminding us that the family we know is one melody among many that the same human materials can be made to play.

passageSets.title: Who Draws the Line
passageSets.passage:
Common sense tells us that some acts are simply deviant, that the criminal, the addict, and the outcast are marked off from the rest of us by what they have done. A long tradition in sociology asks us to entertain a stranger and more unsettling idea: that deviance is not a quality of the act a person commits, but a consequence of the response that act provokes. On this view, no behavior is deviant in itself. It becomes deviant only when others notice it, name it, and treat the doer accordingly. The same act may pass unremarked in one setting and brand a person for life in another. Deviance, in short, is not discovered but conferred, and to understand it we must study not only those who break rules but those with the power to make and apply them.

The strength of this approach appears the moment we notice how selectively the label is applied. Many people break rules; few are caught, and fewer still are publicly branded. Whether an act is overlooked or punished depends heavily on who commits it, against whom, and in what circumstances. The same mischief that is dismissed as a youthful prank in a comfortable suburb becomes, in a poorer neighborhood, the first entry on a criminal record. The powerful are well placed to escape the label and, more importantly, to fix it upon others. Thus the population we call deviant is not a simple sample of all who have transgressed; it is a sample shaped at every step by the judgments of police, courts, and respectable opinion, judgments that fall unevenly across the lines of class and status.

More striking still is the claim that the label does not merely describe deviance but helps to produce it. Once a person has been publicly marked as a thief or a delinquent, the world begins to treat him as one. Doors close; the respectable keep their distance; legitimate opportunities dry up. He finds an easier welcome among others who bear the same mark, and gradually the identity that was thrust upon him becomes the identity he lives by. What began as an isolated act hardens, through the reaction it provoked, into a career. Sociologists call this secondary deviance, and its lesson is sobering: the institutions that set out to suppress wrongdoing may, by the way they brand and exclude the wrongdoer, be among the forces that confirm him in it.

Pressed to its limit, this argument can become an evasion, and intellectual honesty requires marking the boundary. To say that society creates deviance through its reactions is not to pretend that all acts are harmless until labeled, or that the victim of a genuine injury is merely a social construction. Some deeds wound regardless of what anyone chooses to call them. The labeling perspective is most illuminating not as a denial that harm exists but as a warning about how unevenly we notice it, how readily we mistake the powerless for the dangerous, and how easily our efforts to control deviance slide into manufacturing the very outcasts we fear.

The deviant, then, is partly our own creation, summoned into being by the line we draw and the zeal with which we police it. This does not excuse every act that falls on the wrong side of that line. But it should make us look harder at the line itself, and at the hands that hold the pen.

passageSets.title: What Ritual Works Upon
passageSets.passage:
To the impatient rationalist, ritual is the very emblem of human folly: a set of repetitive, often arbitrary actions that accomplish nothing the participants could not achieve more efficiently by other means, or that aim at ends, like influencing the weather or the dead, which no action could achieve at all. If the rain dance does not bring rain, why does it survive? The rationalist concludes that ritual is a relic of ignorance, destined to wither as understanding grows. Sociology suggests that this verdict, however reasonable it sounds, rests on a basic mistake about what rituals are for. To judge a ritual by whether it achieves its stated purpose is to misunderstand the kind of thing it is, rather as one might condemn a handshake for failing to transfer anything material between the hands.

Consider what actually happens when a community performs a ritual. People assemble, often at some cost in time and effort; they move, chant, or fall silent in unison; they direct their attention to the same objects and feel, in the heightened atmosphere of the occasion, a current of shared emotion that no one could generate alone. Whatever the rite claims to be about, this gathering of bodies and feelings is its most reliable effect. In the act of worshipping together, a congregation experiences and renews its own unity; the participants come away not with rain, perhaps, but with a refreshed sense of belonging to something larger than themselves. On this reading, the true object of much religious ritual is the society that performs it, which is thereby reminded of its own existence and recharged in its loyalties.

Ritual performs other labors equally invisible to the literal-minded. Rites of passage, those that attend birth, coming of age, marriage, and death, take the unsettling transitions of a life and give them public form, marking the moment when a child becomes an adult or the living take leave of the dead. Without such markers the transitions would still occur, but the community would lack the means to acknowledge them together and the individual would face them alone. Ritual also gives shape to emotions that might otherwise overwhelm. The prescribed forms of mourning tell the grief-stricken what to do when they would otherwise be paralyzed, channeling raw feeling into bearable, shared action. The action that accomplishes nothing in the rationalist's ledger accomplishes a great deal in the economy of human feeling.

If this is right, then the confident prediction that ritual would fade with the advance of reason was bound to fail, and so it has. Modern, secular societies that pride themselves on outgrowing superstition are in fact saturated with ritual, though they seldom call it that. National holidays, graduations, courtroom procedures, the standing ovation, the moment of silence: each gathers people, choreographs their behavior, and stirs a shared sentiment, performing precisely the social work that older rites performed. We have not abolished ritual; we have merely changed its vocabulary and pretended the change was an abolition. The need that ritual answers is not a residue of ignorance but a permanent feature of social life.

To dismiss ritual as empty, then, is to confuse its surface with its substance. Its actions may be literally idle and its stated aims beyond reach, and yet it does indispensable work: binding the group, marking its passages, and lending form to feelings that would otherwise have none. The wise observer asks not whether a ritual works, but what, beneath its appearances, it is quietly working upon.

passageSets.title: Grooves of the Tongue
passageSets.passage:
Does the language we speak shape the way we think? Few questions in the study of mind and culture have proved more seductive, or more slippery. The intuition behind the affirmative answer is powerful. Languages do not merely attach different sounds to the same fixed set of ideas; they carve the world into different categories, oblige their speakers to mark distinctions others ignore, and fall silent where others insist on precision. A language that requires its speakers to specify, in every sentence, the direction of an event or the source of their knowledge trains attention in a particular direction. From such observations grows the hypothesis of linguistic relativity: that the grammar and vocabulary we inherit are not a neutral vessel for thought but a mold that gives it shape.

In its boldest form, the hypothesis claims that language determines thought, that we are prisoners of our mother tongue, unable even to conceive what it gives us no words to express. This strong version is exciting, and almost certainly false. If our language truly fixed the limits of our thought, we could never notice a distinction it failed to mark, yet speakers do this constantly; they borrow words, coin new ones, and grasp foreign ideas that their own grammar never prepared them for. Translation, though imperfect, is possible, and the very fact that we can explain what another language captures more neatly than ours shows that the thought is available to us even when the convenient word is not. A language may make some ideas easier to think, but it does not wall the others off.

Discard the strong claim, however, and a more modest and durable version remains. Language may not determine what we can think, but it powerfully influences what we think readily, habitually, and without effort. The distinctions our language forces us to make are the ones we notice without trying; the categories it hands us ready-made are the ones we reach for first. A speaker whose language sorts a band of color into two names rather than one will, on average, divide that band a little more quickly, not because the other divisions are unthinkable but because his everyday habits run along the grooves his language has worn. Influence, not imprisonment, is the right word. Language is less a cage than a current, easy to swim with and possible, with effort, to swim against.

This moderate position matters beyond the seminar, because the categories a language supplies are rarely neutral. The words available for kinds of people, kinds of work, or kinds of conduct come freighted with the judgments of those who coined them, and to accept the words uncritically is to absorb the judgments along with them. A vocabulary that offers many flattering terms for one group and only contemptuous ones for another shapes the path of least resistance for thought, making some perceptions effortless and others a labor of deliberate resistance. This is why arguments over words are seldom merely about words. To insist on renaming something is to refuse the worn groove, to make a familiar thought a little harder and an unfamiliar one a little easier to reach.

The truth about language and thought lies, as so often, between the slogans. We are neither the helpless captives of our words nor wholly free of their pull. We inherit a set of grooves and swim, for the most part, along them, but we retain the power to notice the current and, now and then, to strike out against it.

hints.prompt: Which choice is a trap because, if it were true, arguments over words would be pointless?

questions.stem: The author's claim that arguments over words are seldom merely about words depends on the assumption that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the categories a language supplies carry evaluative judgments that influence how people perceive and think

choices.label: B
choices.text: words have fixed meanings that never change over time

choices.label: C
choices.text: people are completely unaware of the words they use

choices.label: D
choices.text: renaming something has no effect on how it is perceived

questions.explanation: A is correct because the claim follows only if vocabulary is value-laden, so that disputes over terms are really disputes over the judgments and perceptions they carry; the passage argues that words come freighted with the judgments of those who coined them. B is wrong because the author emphasizes that words and usage can change through coining and renaming, so fixed meaning is not assumed. C is wrong because the argument about deliberate renaming presupposes awareness of word choice, not unawareness. D is wrong because if renaming had no effect, arguments over words would be pointless, the opposite of the author's claim that renaming can shift the path of least resistance for thought.

questions.stem: In context, the author's statement that language is 'less a cage than a current' most nearly means that language:

choices.label: A
choices.text: physically traps thought within rigid bars

choices.label: B
choices.text: guides and eases thought in certain directions without making other thoughts impossible

choices.label: C
choices.text: has no bearing whatsoever on the direction of thought

choices.label: D
choices.text: flows too quickly for anyone to resist

questions.explanation: B is correct because a current is easy to swim with and possible, with effort, to swim against, capturing influence without imprisonment, which is the author's moderate thesis. A is wrong because less a cage explicitly denies the trapping, deterministic image. C is wrong because a current still exerts a pull, so the phrase affirms influence, not its absence. D is wrong because the author says one can swim against the current with effort, so it is not presented as irresistible.
